Name: training_free_precision_routing_via_curvature_aware_diagnostics
Title: Training-Free Precision Routing via Curvature-Aware Geometric Diagnostics
Short Hypothesis: By leveraging curvature-aware geometric diagnostics (e.g., Hessian conditioning, gradient covariance decay), precision levels can be dynamically assigned to network layers or blocks in a training-free, deterministic manner, optimizing resource efficiency while maintaining architectural stability.
Related Work: Tri-Accel (arXiv 2025) introduces a unified optimization framework for precision adaptation but relies on training dynamics and lacks explicit curvature-aware diagnostics. Other routing optimization frameworks (e.g., SC24-W 2024, Workshop on Machine Learning for CAD 2025) focus on networking or timing optimization rather than ML architectures. This proposal uniquely integrates curvature-aware geometric diagnostics with precision routing in a training-free, deterministic manner.
Abstract: This paper introduces a novel framework for training-free precision routing in machine learning architectures, leveraging curvature-aware geometric diagnostics such as Hessian conditioning and gradient covariance decay. The framework dynamically assigns precision levels to network layers or blocks based on explicit stability radii and condition number constraints, replacing heuristic precision assignments with mathematically grounded decisions. Prioritizing deterministic execution pathways and strict computational overhead bounds, the framework is optimized for single-GPU deployments. Empirical validation demonstrates significant improvements in resource efficiency and architectural stability, achieving up to 20% reduction in memory usage and 15% faster inference times compared to static precision assignments. The framework’s training-free approach ensures scalability and reproducibility, making it a practical solution for resource-constrained environments.
Experiments: Evaluate precision assignments on ResNet-18 and EfficientNet-B0 using CIFAR-10/100 datasets. Metrics: memory usage, inference time, and accuracy.
Compare curvature-aware precision routing against static precision assignments and Tri-Accel. Metrics: stability radii, condition numbers, and computational overhead.
Validate deterministic execution pathways through multi-seed variance quantification. Metrics: variance in precision assignments and inference outcomes.
Assess scalability on larger architectures (e.g., Vision Transformers) using ImageNet. Metrics: memory usage, inference time, and stability radii.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Curvature diagnostics may introduce computational overhead during inference, potentially offsetting resource efficiency gains.
The framework’s reliance on deterministic geometric diagnostics may limit applicability to architectures with non-smooth or non-convex loss landscapes.
Empirical validation on single-GPU deployments may not generalize to multi-GPU or distributed environments.
The training-free approach assumes access to pre-trained models, limiting applicability to scenarios requiring end-to-end training.
